module.title: HASHES & SYMBOLS
module.subtitle: hash literals · symbol keys · fetch · tally
module.theory:
## What is a hash?
A **hash** is a lookup table: it maps **keys** to **values**. Think of it as a labelled drawer — instead of asking for "item number 3" (an array index), you ask for the drawer named `:bpm`.
~~~ruby
track = { name: "Da Funk", bpm: 112, key: "Am" }
track[:bpm]   # 112
~~~
The **WHY**: arrays are great when order matters, but when you want to look something up *by name* — a config value, a player's score, a count — a hash is faster and clearer.

## Symbol keys
Those `name:` / `bpm:` keys are **symbols**. A symbol like `:bpm` is a lightweight, immutable label — written with a leading colon. The literal `{ bpm: 112 }` is just shorthand for `{ :bpm => 112 }`.
~~~ruby
{ bpm: 112 }          # symbol key, the modern style
{ :bpm => 112 }       # exactly the same hash
{ "bpm" => 112 }      # a STRING key — a DIFFERENT key!
~~~

> WARNING — `:bpm` (symbol) and `"bpm"` (string) are **not** the same key. `h[:bpm]` will return **nil** if the hash was built with string keys. When you write `{ bpm: 112 }`, the key is the *symbol* `:bpm`, so read it back with `h[:bpm]`.

## Reading: `[ ]` vs `fetch`
Two ways to read a value:
~~~ruby
track[:bpm]              # 112
track[:genre]            # nil  (missing key -> nil, no error)
track.fetch(:bpm)        # 112
track.fetch(:genre, 0)   # 0    (missing key -> the default you pass)
track.fetch(:genre)      # raises KeyError!  (no default given)
~~~
Use **`h[:k]`** for a quick read where `nil` is fine. Use **`h.fetch(:k, default)`** when a missing key should fall back to a sensible value, or **`h.fetch(:k)`** when a missing key is a real bug you want to blow up loudly.

## Iterating
Walk every pair with **`each`** (or its twin **`each_pair`**). The block gets the key and the value:
~~~ruby
scores = { ren: 3, lain: 5 }
scores.each { |name, points| puts "#{name}: #{points}" }
# ren: 3
# lain: 5
~~~
Need just one side? **`scores.keys`** gives `[:ren, :lain]`, **`scores.values`** gives `[3, 5]`.

## Counting with `Hash.new(0)`
A plain `{}` returns `nil` for unknown keys, so `count[x] += 1` would crash (`nil + 1`). Give the hash a **default value** with `Hash.new(0)` — now any unseen key reads as `0`, perfect for tallies:
~~~ruby
tally = Hash.new(0)
["a", "b", "a"].each { |c| tally[c] += 1 }
tally   # { "a" => 2, "b" => 1 }
~~~

> INTEL — `Hash.new(0)` is the single most useful hash trick: it turns counting into a one-liner. The `0` is the value handed back for any key you never set.

## Combining: `merge`
**`merge`** returns a **new** hash with both sets of pairs; on a clash, the **argument wins**. It does **not** change the original.
~~~ruby
defaults = { hp: 100, mp: 30 }
defaults.merge({ mp: 50, lvl: 9 })   # { hp: 100, mp: 50, lvl: 9 }
defaults                             # { hp: 100, mp: 30 }  (untouched)
~~~

> WARNING — `merge` returns the combined hash — it does **not** modify the receiver. The common beginner mistake is calling `a.merge(b)` and expecting `a` to change. Capture the result: `merged = a.merge(b)` (or use `merge!` only when you truly mean to mutate). And remember Ruby's implicit return — the **last expression** is the method's value, so write the hash last instead of reaching for an explicit `return`.

exercises.title: DRAWER READ
exercises.brief: Pull one value out of a hash.
exercises.prompt:
A **track** is a hash with symbol keys like **{ name: "Spice", bpm: 128 }**. Define **get_bpm(track)** returning the value under the **:bpm** key.
~~~ruby
get_bpm({ name: "Spice", bpm: 128 })  # 128
~~~
exercises.starter:
def get_bpm(track)
  # TODO: read the :bpm value
  0
end
exercises.hint: track[:bpm]  — note the leading colon, it's a symbol key.
exercises.lore: Open the drawer marked bpm. Read what's inside.

exercises.title: SAFE FETCH
exercises.brief: Read a key with a fallback.
exercises.prompt:
Define **volume_of(settings)** returning the **:volume** value from the **settings** hash, or **5** if that key is missing. Use **fetch** with a default.
~~~ruby
volume_of({ volume: 9 })  # 9
volume_of({})             # 5
~~~
exercises.starter:
def volume_of(settings)
  # TODO: fetch :volume, default 5
  settings[:volume]
end
exercises.hint: settings.fetch(:volume, 5)  — the second argument is returned when the key is absent.
exercises.lore: No signal on the line? Fall back to a known-safe level.

exercises.title: NAME ROSTER
exercises.brief: List the keys of a hash.
exercises.prompt:
**roster** is a hash mapping each callsign (a symbol) to a score, like **{ ren: 3, lain: 5 }**. Define **callsigns(roster)** returning an **array of just the keys**, in order.
~~~ruby
callsigns({ ren: 3, lain: 5 })  # [:ren, :lain]
~~~
exercises.starter:
def callsigns(roster)
  # TODO: return the hash's keys
  []
end
exercises.hint: roster.keys returns the keys as an array; roster.values would give the scores.
exercises.lore: Who's on the net tonight? Read the roster.

exercises.title: LEDGER TOTAL
exercises.brief: Add up every value in the hash.
exercises.prompt:
**ledger** maps a name to a number, like **{ spike: 25, jet: 10 }**. Define **total(ledger)** returning the **sum of all the values**. An empty ledger totals **0**. Reach for **values**.
~~~ruby
total({ spike: 25, jet: 10 })  # 35
total({})                      # 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
def total(ledger)
  # TODO: sum the values
  nil
end
exercises.hint: ledger.values.sum  — pull the numbers out, then fold them.
exercises.lore: Every bounty on the board, added head by head.

exercises.title: CONFIG OVERLAY
exercises.brief: Patch defaults with overrides, no mutation.
exercises.prompt:
Define **apply(defaults, overrides)** returning a **new** hash with all the pairs of both, where **overrides** wins on any shared key. Do **not** change **defaults**. Use **merge**.
~~~ruby
apply({ hp: 100, mp: 30 }, { mp: 50, lvl: 9 })  # { hp: 100, mp: 50, lvl: 9 }
~~~
exercises.starter:
def apply(defaults, overrides)
  # TODO: merge, letting overrides win
  defaults
end

tests.name: overrides win
tests.code: assert_equal(apply({ hp: 100, mp: 30 }, { mp: 50, lvl: 9 }), { hp: 100, mp: 50, lvl: 9 })

tests.name: does not mutate defaults

exercises.hint: defaults.merge(overrides) returns a fresh hash; on a clash the argument's value wins.
exercises.lore: Defaults are a suggestion. The overlay is law.

exercises.title: PACKET TALLY
exercises.brief: Count how often each item appears.
exercises.prompt:
Define **tally(items)** taking an array and returning a hash mapping **each item to how many times it appears**. Build it with **Hash.new(0)** and **each**.
~~~ruby
tally(["a", "b", "a"])  # { "a" => 2, "b" => 1 }
~~~
exercises.hint: Hash.new(0) makes every unseen key read as 0, so counts[x] += 1 just works.
exercises.lore: Every packet logged. The pattern hides in the counts.

exercises.title: DOSSIER LINES
exercises.brief: Turn each pair into a formatted line.
exercises.prompt:
**dossier** maps a symbol field to a string value, like **{ name: "Lain", city: "Tokyo" }**. Define **lines(dossier)** returning an **array of strings** — one per pair, formatted **"key: value"** — in order. Iterate with **each_pair** (or **each**).
~~~ruby
lines({ name: "Lain", city: "Tokyo" })  # ["name: Lain", "city: Tokyo"]
~~~
exercises.starter:
def lines(dossier)
  out = []
  # TODO: each_pair, push "#{key}: #{value}"
  out
end
exercises.hint: dossier.each_pair { |key, value| out << "#{key}: #{value}" } — the symbol key interpolates without its colon.
exercises.lore: The dossier reads itself, line by line.

exercises.title: TOP CALLSIGN
exercises.brief: Print the highest-scoring entry from a tally.
exercises.prompt:
This is a **script** task. Start from this scoreboard exactly:
~~~ruby
scores = { "ren" => 3, "lain" => 5, "akira" => 1 }
~~~
Walk every pair with **each**, find the callsign with the **highest** score (assume no ties), and **puts** that callsign on its own line.
~~~text
lain
~~~
exercises.starter:
scores = { "ren" => 3, "lain" => 5, "akira" => 1 }
best = scores.keys.first
# TODO: walk the pairs and keep the callsign with the most points
puts best
exercises.solution:
scores = { "ren" => 3, "lain" => 5, "akira" => 1 }
best = nil
best_points = -1
scores.each do |name, points|
  if points > best_points
    best = name
    best_points = points
  end
end
puts best

tests.name: does not print a loser

exercises.hint: Track a running best and best_points; replace both whenever points is larger.
exercises.lore: One name rises above the net. Surface it.
